title: Visual Character Delete
goal: Delete `TODO` using visual mode with `v`, `e`, then `d`.
explanation: Visual mode is ideal when you can see the range and want an immediate operation.
hints: Press v to enter visual mode.
Move to the end of TODO with e.
Press d to delete the selected text.
rule: Use visual mode when selecting and operating on a region is clearer than building an operator motion.

check.success_text: You selected and deleted a visual range.

title: Visual Line Yank And Paste
goal: Yank the middle line with `V y`, move down, then paste with `p`.
explanation: Linewise visual mode is useful for copy-and-reorder tasks.
hints: Use V to enter visual-line mode.
Press y to yank the line.
Move down with j and paste with p.
rule: Linewise yanks are useful when structure matters more than exact columns.

check.success_text: You yanked a full line in visual mode and pasted it.

title: Change Inside Quotes
goal: Replace `pending` with `done` using `ci"`.
explanation: Quote text objects let you edit values without touching the surrounding syntax.
hints: Place the cursor inside the quoted text.
Use c i " then type done and Esc.
rule: Text objects are safer than manual cursor movement for structured edits.

check.success_text: You changed only the text inside the quotes.

title: Delete Inside Parentheses
goal: Use `di(` to remove function arguments from `deploy(api, true)`.
explanation: Parenthesis text objects are core for editing function calls and signatures.
rule: Use inner objects (`i`) when you want to preserve delimiters.

check.success_text: You removed only the content inside the parentheses.

title: Delete Around Paragraph
goal: Use `dap` to remove the first paragraph and the separator line.
explanation: Paragraph objects are useful for prose and commit message cleanup.
rule: Around objects (`a`) include surrounding separators when appropriate.

check.success_text: You deleted a paragraph as a structural unit.

title: Jumplist Back And Forward
goal: Search `deploy`, move to next hit, then use Ctrl-o and Ctrl-i to move through jump history.
explanation: Jumplist navigation lets you explore aggressively and still return safely.
rule: Jump history is a navigation safety net for large files.

check.success_text: You used jumplist navigation to move back and forward through jumps.

title: Change List Navigation
goal: Make edits on two lines, then use `g;` to jump to an older change.
explanation: Changelist jumps are useful when revisiting recent edits quickly.
hints: Delete one char with x, move down, delete another with x.
rule: Use changelist jumps when reviewing or finishing recent edits.

check.success_text: You jumped back to an older change location.

title: Named Register Reuse
goal: Yank the first line into register a, jump to the bottom, then paste it.
explanation: Named registers let you keep reusable text while performing other edits.
rule: Use named registers when you need to preserve text beyond one operation.

check.success_text: You stored text in a named register and reused it later.

title: Quickfix Iterate Matches
goal: Run :vimgrep /TODO/ % and jump to the next hit with :cnext.
explanation: Quickfix turns global matches into an actionable navigation list.
rule: Use quickfix when you need to process many search hits efficiently.

check.success_text: You moved through a quickfix result set.

title: Explorer Parent Navigation
goal: Open explorer at /tmp, move to parent with -, then exit with Esc.
explanation: Netrw is a lightweight built-in explorer, useful even without plugins.
rule: Know the built-in explorer so you can navigate files even in minimal setups.

check.success_text: You opened explorer, navigated to parent, and exited cleanly.

title: Plugin Workflow Literacy
goal: Run :Lazy sync, then :Mason, then :TSUpdate.
explanation: You do not need to memorize one plugin manager, but you should recognize common maintenance flows.
rule: Operational literacy matters: know what maintenance commands look like and when to run them.

check.success_text: You ran the core plugin maintenance commands.

title: LSP Info And Definition Jump
goal: Run :LspInfo, then jump to deploy definition with gd.
explanation: LSP workflows are anchored by two habits: inspect client state and jump to definitions quickly.
hints: Move cursor over deploy and use gd.
rule: Use :LspInfo for diagnosis and gd for core navigation.

check.success_text: You inspected LSP status and jumped to a definition.

title: Telescope Picker Basics
goal: Open :Telescope find_files, exit with Esc, then run :Telescope buffers.
explanation: Telescope pickers are fast entry points for file, text, and buffer navigation.
hints: Press Esc to close picker/explorer mode.
rule: Treat pickers as command interfaces for navigation, not just UI widgets.

check.success_text: You used two common Telescope pickers in sequence.

title: Safety And Recovery Commands
goal: Run :wa, then :qa!, then :recover.
explanation: Safe editing includes understanding global write, force quit, and recovery flows.
rule: Know the safety commands before you need them in a stressful situation.

check.success_text: You practiced the core safety and recovery commands.

title: Profile And Inspect
goal: Start profiling, stop profiling, then inspect script load order with :scriptnames.
explanation: Performance debugging starts with collecting profile data and inspecting runtime context.
rule: Measure first, then inspect runtime context before making performance changes.

check.success_text: You started and stopped profiling, then inspected script loading.

title: Indent A Line
goal: Indent the second line with `>>`, then outdent it back with `<<`.
explanation: `>>` shifts the current line right by one indent unit. `<<` shifts left. They're operator-style commands that respect counts (e.g. `3>>`).
hints: Move down to the second line with j.
rule: Use >> / << to nudge a single line in or out by one shift width.

check.success_text: You indented and then outdented the line back to its starting column.

title: Indent A Visual Block
goal: Select all three lines with `V` then `2j`, then indent them with `>`.
explanation: In linewise visual mode, > and < shift every selected line by one indent unit at once.
hints: Press V to enter visual-line mode.
Press 2j to extend the selection to the third line.
Press > to indent the whole selection.
rule: Visual selections turn shift commands into bulk indents.

check.success_text: You used a visual-line selection to indent multiple lines in one stroke.

title: Delete By Word And Line In Insert
goal: From Insert mode, use `<C-w>` to delete a word backward, then `<C-u>` to clear the rest of the line.
explanation: Inside Insert mode, <C-w> deletes the previous word and <C-u> deletes from the cursor back to the start of the line. Both save you from leaving Insert just to fix a typo.
hints: You start in Insert mode at the end of a draft line.
Press <C-w> to remove the trailing word.
Press <C-u> to clear the line from the cursor to its start.
rule: Insert-mode line/word deletes mean you don't have to bounce back to Normal for small fixes.

check.success_text: You used <C-w> and <C-u> to clear the line without leaving Insert mode.

title: One Normal Command In Insert
goal: From Insert mode, use `<C-o>` followed by `dw` to delete the word ahead, all without exiting Insert.
explanation: <C-o> lets you run a single Normal-mode command and bounce straight back to Insert. Perfect when you need a quick fix mid-typing.
hints: You start in Insert mode at the start of `replace me now`.
Press <C-o>, then dw to delete the next word.
You stay in Insert mode after — no Esc needed.
rule: <C-o> is your escape hatch from Insert mode that doesn't actually leave Insert mode.

check.success_text: You ran a Normal-mode delete from inside Insert mode and stayed there.

title: Delete A Line Range
goal: Delete lines 2 through 4 with `:2,4d`.
explanation: Ex commands accept ranges as a prefix: a single number, a comma-separated pair, `%` for the whole buffer, or `.` and `$` for current and last.
hints: Lines 2 through 4 disappear in one shot.
rule: Ranges turn a single ex command into bulk action without macros or visual selection.

check.success_text: You used a numeric range to delete a slice of the buffer.

title: Rename With cgn And Dot
goal: Rename every `foo` to `bar` using `/foo`, `cgn`, then `.`.
explanation: `cgn` changes the next search match. `.` repeats that change. Together they're the canonical Vim refactor: search once, change once, repeat.
hints: Use cgn, type bar, press Esc — you've replaced one match.
Press . to repeat the change on the next match.
Repeat . until every foo is gone.
rule: Search once, change once with cgn, then dot-repeat across the file.

check.success_text: You renamed every match by combining cgn with dot-repeat — the heart of Vim refactoring.

title: Macro Across A Quickfix List
goal: Record a macro that turns `TODO` into `DONE`, populate quickfix with `:vimgrep /TODO/ %`, then run the macro on each match.
explanation: Macros + quickfix is the move that makes Vim feel limitless. Record once on the first match, vimgrep the rest, and replay across the list.
hints: Start on the first TODO at row 0.
Record a macro: qa, then cwDONE Esc, then q to stop.
Use :cnext to jump to the next match, then @a to replay the macro. Repeat until all TODOs are DONE.
canonical_solutions: qa cwDONE Esc q :vimgrep /TODO/ % Enter :cnext Enter @a :cnext Enter @a
rule: Record the transform once, navigate matches with quickfix, replay the macro on every hit.

check.success_text: You combined a recorded macro with a quickfix list to apply the same edit across every match.
